title: Oeufs en meurette
subtitle: Eggs poached in red wine sauce with lardons and croutons
excerpt: A Burgundian bistro classic: eggs poached in a red wine sauce built with lardons, chestnut mushrooms, shallots, and a splash of stock. The sauce is deep, glossy, and slightly sweet from the red wine reduction. Served in shallow bowls with croutons, it is one of the great French first courses.
sourceNotes: Auguste Escoffier, Le Guide Culinaire (1903), public domain. Oeufs pochés en meurette method, meurette sauce composition with red wine, lardons, and button onions. Henri-Paul Pellaprat, L'Art culinaire moderne (1935), public domain via archive.org. Burgundian poached egg preparation including crouton garnish and sauce reduction technique.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: The meurette sauce can be made up to 2 days ahead and refrigerated. Reheat gently and poach the eggs fresh just before serving.

glossaryTerms.definition: A Burgundian red wine sauce made with lardons, pearl onions (or shallots), mushrooms, and stock. Used as a braising and poaching liquid for eggs, fish, and meats. The name comes from the Burgundian word for brine or saltwater; the sauce is a staple of the bistro table in the region.

To cook food gently in a liquid that is below boiling point. For eggs, the liquid is kept at a bare simmer (around 85°C to 90°C) so the white sets gently without toughening. A small amount of vinegar in the water helps the white coagulate quickly around the yolk.
A small cube or slice of bread fried or baked in butter or oil until golden and crisp. Used as a garnish for soups, salads, and egg dishes. The French word means 'little crust'.

content.text: in a rich Burgundian red wine sauce and served with

. The meurette is a complete sauce: lardons and chestnut mushrooms cooked in red wine with shallots and stock until the sauce is dark, glossy, and deeply flavoured. The egg, with its soft yolk, provides the richness that ties it together.

This is a dish of two parts: the sauce and the eggs. The sauce can be made well ahead; the eggs must be poached at the last minute. Keep the sauce at a steady simmer when poaching; a rolling boil breaks up the whites.
Use a Burgundy if you can, or any full-bodied red wine. Cheap, thin wine makes a thin sauce. The wine is the base of the whole dish.

items.prepNote: a Burgundy or other full-bodied red
day-old white bread, cut into 1 cm cubes

content.text: Use a wide, shallow saucepan or deep frying pan for poaching the eggs so they can be lowered in gently and removed easily. A slotted spoon is essential.

Cook the {{lardons}} of lardons in a saucepan over medium heat for 4 to 5 minutes until coloured. Remove with a slotted spoon and set aside. Add the {{shallot}} to the fat in the pan and cook for 3 minutes. Add the {{garlic}} of garlic and stir for 1 minute. Add the {{chestnut-mushrooms}} of chestnut mushrooms and cook for 3 minutes. Sprinkle over the {{plain-flour}} of flour and stir well to coat the vegetables. Pour in the {{red-wine}} of red wine and stir, scraping the base. Add the {{stock-beef}} of beef stock, {{thyme}} of thyme, and {{bay-leaves}} of bay. Return the lardons to the pan. Simmer for 20 minutes until the sauce has reduced by about a third and has a glossy, coating consistency. Discard the herb sprigs and bay leaves. Off the heat, whisk in the cold {{unsalted-butter}} of butter in pieces. Taste and season.

While the sauce reduces, melt the second portion of {{unsalted-butter}} of butter in a frying pan over medium-high heat. Add the bread cubes from the {{bread}} slices of bread and fry for 3 to 4 minutes, turning frequently, until golden on all sides. Drain on kitchen paper and keep warm.

Bring a wide, shallow pan of water to a steady simmer. Add the {{cider-vinegar}} of cider vinegar. Stir to create a gentle swirl. Break each of the {{eggs}} eggs into a small cup, then slide it into the water near the edge. Cook 2 at a time to keep the pan temperature stable. Poach for 3 to 4 minutes until the whites are set and the yolks are still soft. Remove with a slotted spoon and drain briefly on kitchen paper.
Ladle the hot meurette sauce into warmed shallow bowls. Place 2 poached eggs in each bowl. Scatter the croutons and the {{parsley-flat}} of flat-leaf parsley over the top. Serve immediately.

items.symptom: Egg whites are ragged and stringy
items.cause: Water boiling too fast, or eggs not fresh enough
items.fix: Keep the water at a gentle simmer, not a rolling boil; very fresh eggs poach more cleanly because the whites are tighter. Adding vinegar to the water helps

items.cause: Not reduced long enough, or too much stock added
items.fix: Simmer uncovered for a few more minutes; the sauce should coat the back of a spoon before the butter goes in

items.symptom: Sauce is too thick and sticky
items.fix: Add a splash of stock or water and stir; taste and adjust the seasoning which will have concentrated too
3 to 4 minutes is the window for a soft yolk; 4.5 to 5 minutes gives a yolk that is just beginning to set at the edges. Check by pressing the egg gently with your finger

items.symptom: Eggs stick to the pan base
items.cause: Not enough water, or water not hot enough before eggs were added
items.fix: Use a wide, deep pan with plenty of water; make sure it is simmering before adding the eggs; a light stir creates a current that stops the eggs settling

content.text: Oeufs en meurette is a dish of Burgundy, the wine region centred on Dijon. The meurette sauce takes its name from an old Burgundian word for saltwater or brine; the sauce itself uses the region's most celebrated ingredient. It appears on bistro menus throughout the region as a first course, often served alongside a glass of the same wine used in the sauce. It is also made with freshwater fish (pochouse, a Burgundian fish stew) using the same sauce base.
